items.title: Ridge alpha tuning without overfitting one validation slice
items.scenario: A team has 600 house-price rows and no separate validation block—only train (540) and locked test (60). They run 5-fold CV on the 540 rows for α ∈ {0.01, 0.1, 1, 10}. α=0.01 mean RMSE 0.48 (std 0.04); α=10 mean 0.41 (std 0.03). They pick α=10, refit on all 540, then report test RMSE once.
items.explanation: CV averages tuning across five partitions instead of trusting one 80/20 validation cut. The test set stays untouched until after α is chosen.

items.title: Random forest depth: mean beats a lucky single split
items.scenario: On 1,200 churn labels, a single hold-out favored `max_depth=20` (AUC 0.87) over depth 8 (0.84). Stratified 5-fold CV shows depth 8 mean AUC 0.86 (std 0.01) vs depth 20 mean 0.85 (std 0.05). They deploy depth 8.
items.explanation: The shallow forest wins on stability across folds—the deep forest overfit one validation slice. CV for model selection surfaces variance-driven overfitting.

items.title: Nested CV after a suspicious GridSearchCV winner
items.scenario: A researcher grid-searches 40 SVM configs with 5-fold CV on 400 patients and reports best mean CV AUC 0.92. Nested outer 5-fold / inner 3-fold yields outer mean AUC 0.86. The gap reveals tuning optimism on the same folds.
items.explanation: Inner CV picked the best of many settings on shared partitions. Outer nested scores estimate generalization without that selection bias.

items.title: Comparing Ridge vs Lasso on sparse genomics features
items.scenario: With p=2,000 features and n=180 samples, a single validation split favored Lasso (F1 0.71). 10-fold stratified CV: Ridge mean F1 0.68 (std 0.02), Lasso mean 0.67 (std 0.06). They choose Ridge for deployment stability.
items.explanation: Small n makes one split unreliable. CV mitigates overfitting to partition luck when choosing between regularized linear models.
